perturbation.original_text: B. Contractor is, and will remain, an independent contractor in with our relationship to the Company. C. The Company shall not be responsible for withholding taxes with respect to our compensation. Contractor agrees to indemnify, defend and hold the Company harmless from any liability for, or assessment of, any claims or penalties with respect to such withholding taxes, labor or employment requirements, including any liability for, or assessment of, withholding taxes imposed on the Company by the relevant taxing authorities with respect to any compensation paid to Contractor.
perturbation.changed_text: B. Contractor is, and will remain, an independent contractor in with our relationship to the Company. C. The Company shall be responsible for withholding taxes with respect to our compensation. Contractor agrees to indemnify, defend and hold the Company harmless from any liability for, or assessment of, any claims or penalties with respect to such taxes.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the company is responsible for withholding taxes for an independent contractor, we are creating a situation that implies an employer-employee relationship, which contradicts the stated independent contractor status.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 3402 - Income tax collected at source
perturbation.law_explanation: This code states that an employer should deduct income taxes from an employee. This contradicts the independent contractor agreement, as employers are not required to deduct taxes from payments to independent contractors.

Any tables or procedures prescribed under
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text correctly identifies the contractor as independent and states the company is not responsible for withholding taxes. The changed text then says "The Company shall be responsible for withholding taxes with respect to our compensation." This directly contradicts the independent contractor status initially established, because withholding taxes is something done for employees, not independent contractors. 26 USC 3402 states that "every employer making payment of wages shall deduct and withhold upon such wages a tax". Thus, assigning the company responsibility for withholding taxes implies an employer-employee relationship, directly contradicting the stated independent contractor arrangement. This is a clear and direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: A. Confidentiality and Confidential Information . Contractor agrees to regard and preserve as confidential all information obtained by Contractor relating or pertaining to (i) the Company’s business, projects, plans, products, planned or proposed products, customers, potential customers, trade secrets, and other confidential information (including business and financial information), and any computer programs and software or unpublished know-how, whether patented or unpatented, and to (ii) all of our activities for or on behalf of the Company, and Contractor agrees not to publish or disclose any part of such information to others or use the same for our own purposes or the purposes of others, during the time Contractor is working for Company or thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: A. Confidentiality and Confidential Information . Contractor agrees to regard and preserve as confidential all information obtained by Contractor relating or pertaining to (i) the Company’s business, projects, plans, products, planned or proposed products, customers, potential customers, trade secrets, and other confidential information (including business and financial information), and any computer programs and software or unpublished know-how, whether patented or unpatented, and to (ii) all of our activities for or on behalf of the Company, and Contractor agrees not to publish or disclose any part of such information to others or use the same for our own purposes or the purposes of others, during the time Contractor is working for Company, but may disclose after the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the Contractor to disclose confidential information after the contract is over, directly contradicting the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement, which is to prevent the disclosure of confidential information even after the business relationship has ended.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA protects trade secrets indefinitely. Allowing disclosure post-termination directly conflicts with the ongoing protection that CUTSA provides to trade secrets, making the contract unenforceable to that extent.

perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Contractor to disclose confidential information after the contract terminates, which contradicts the purpose of protecting trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that CUTSA protects trade secrets indefinitely, and allowing disclosure post-termination directly conflicts with this ongoing protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the relevant legislation as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CIV 3426), indicating the modification undermines the protections this law provides by allowing disclosure of trade secrets after the agreement ends, which is when protection should still apply.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor hereby assigns, and agree to assign, to the Company all our right, title and interest in and to all Company Inventions. Also, Contractor hereby assigns, and agree to assign, to the Company all Inventions conceived or reduced to practice by Contractor within one year following the termination of our work for the Company (voluntary or otherwise), if the Invention is a result of the Company’s information which was obtained by Contractor during our work for the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor hereby assigns, and agree to assign, to the Company all our right, title and interest in and to all Company Inventions. Also, Contractor hereby assigns, and agree to assign, to the Company all Inventions conceived or reduced to practice by Contractor within five years following the termination of our work for the Company (voluntary or otherwise), if the Invention is a result of the Company’s information which was obtained by Contractor during our work for the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the assignment period for inventions post-termination to five years may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially in California, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While there are exceptions for sale of business or partnership dissolutions, a five-year post-employment invention assignment could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, conflicting with this statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the post-termination invention assignment period to five years, which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as explained by both the Perturbation Explanation and the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." A five-year assignment period could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint of trade, making the modified clause likely unenforceable under California law. The original text's one-year period is less likely to be seen as an unreasonable restraint compared to the modified five-year period. The modified text creates a legal contradiction because it increases the risk of violating BPC 16600.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor agrees not to compete with the Company as follows: A. During The Time Contractor is Working for the Company . Contractor agrees that during the time Contractor is working for the Company, Contractor will not interfere with the business of the Company in any manner. Particularly, but without limitation, Contractor agrees to refrain from planning or organizing a competitive business during the time Contractor is working for the Company. Contractor further agrees that during the time Contractor is working for the Company, Contractor will not have any proprietary interest in any competitive business except for an interest of less than five percent (5%) of the outstanding shares of a publicly-held corporation, meaning a corporation whose outstanding shares are owned by one hundred (100) or more shareholders. Contractor further agrees that during the time Contractor is working for the Company, Contractor will not solicit customers of the Company for any purpose whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor agrees not to compete with the Company as follows: A. Both During and After The Time Contractor is Working for the Company . Contractor agrees that during the time Contractor is working for the Company, and for five years after leaving the company, Contractor will not interfere with the business of the Company in any manner. Particularly, but without limitation, Contractor agrees to refrain from planning or organizing a competitive business. Contractor further agrees that Contractor will not have any proprietary interest in any competitive business except for an interest of less than five percent (5%) of the outstanding shares of a publicly-held corporation, meaning a corporation whose outstanding shares are owned by one hundred (100) or more shareholders. Contractor further agrees that Contractor will not solicit customers of the Company for any purpose whatsoever for a period of 5 years after leaving the company.
perturbation.explanation: This change adds a non-compete clause that extends for five years after the contract ends. In California, such broad non-compete clauses are generally unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The added language, preventing competition for five years after termination, directly violates this statute.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a non-compete clause extending five years post-employment, which directly contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The added restriction on the contractor's ability to compete after leaving the company constitutes such a restraint, thus creating a legal contradiction. The snippet further clarifies in (b)(1) that this section should be read broadly to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter.
